title: Freddy Spaghetti

jokes.text: The government is shut down. It's in every newspaper. / How long is it going to last? Well, if we're lucky, this building will be empty for months.
jokes.setup: Andy asks how long the shutdown will last, expecting concern
jokes.punchline: Ron hopes it lasts months - opposite reaction from what you'd expect from a government employee
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron Swanson character moment - his libertarian anti-government stance makes him happy about the shutdown

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: How long do you think the shutdown will last?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I hope it lasts for months. Shutting down the government is like my birthday and Christmas had a baby.

jokes.text: I just got this super sweet-ass rad crotch rocket... Got a really good deal on my lease. Paying 12% interest. It's, like, one of the highest you can get.
jokes.setup: Andy excitedly talks about getting a great deal on a motorcycle
jokes.punchline: He's proud of having one of the highest interest rates possible
jokes.explanation: Classic Andy financial illiteracy - he thinks a terrible deal is great

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I got an amazing deal on this motorcycle! 24% interest rate!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Do you know how rare it is to get financing that good? That's basically the best rate out there!

jokes.text: Uh-oh! / Nailed it.
jokes.setup: Andy says 'Uh-oh' suggesting something went wrong
jokes.punchline: He immediately follows with 'Nailed it' - celebrating failure
jokes.explanation: Short and sweet contradiction, perfectly delivered

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Uh-oh.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Nailed it.

jokes.text: Not like it's a competition or anything. Because if it was, I would win. But that's neither here nor there. The point is I would win.
jokes.setup: Leslie says being upset isn't a competition
jokes.punchline: She immediately can't help but insist she'd win anyway, twice
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie competitiveness - she can't even make a point without turning it into a competition

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Being upset isn't a competition.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: But if it were, I would win. Twice.

jokes.text: With the government shut down, who is going to stop Al-Qaeda?
jokes.setup: Concerned citizen asks about park closures
jokes.punchline: Jumps to international terrorism as the consequence
jokes.explanation: Absurd escalation from local parks to national security

dialogue.speaker: Citizen
dialogue.line: So you're telling me the parks are closing? What's next, international terrorism?

jokes.text: Keep them in my house? Where I live?
jokes.setup: Parent asks what to do with kids when parks are closed
jokes.punchline: The horror of keeping them in her own home
jokes.explanation: Absurd parental priorities - home is treated as unthinkable option

dialogue.speaker: Parent
dialogue.line: What am I supposed to do with my kids when the parks are closed?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Well, you could keep them in your own home.

dialogue.speaker: Parent
dialogue.line: The horror!

jokes.text: I don't like having Freddy Spaghetti's blood on my hands. / I believe you mean Freddy Spaghetti's sauce.
jokes.setup: Leslie dramatically mentions blood on her hands
jokes.punchline: Councilman corrects it to sauce, staying in the pasta theme
jokes.explanation: Simple wordplay on the pasta performer's name

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I have blood on my hands.

dialogue.speaker: Councilman Milton
dialogue.line: That's marinara sauce.

jokes.text: Burned my tongue. Don't even care.
jokes.setup: Leslie is drinking something hot
jokes.punchline: Her dedication overcomes physical pain
jokes.explanation: Shows Leslie's single-minded determination in a small moment

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: This coffee is way too hot, but I'm going to drink it anyway because I have a meeting in two minutes and I need the caffeine.

jokes.text: I'd like some water. I'd like it to be cold. I'd like it without ice. I'd like it in a glass or a mug with no handle.
jokes.setup: Chris asks for water
jokes.punchline: Increasingly specific and contradictory requirements
jokes.explanation: Chris's perfectionist personality shown through absurd water specifications

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Can I get some water?

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Actually, make it room temperature. No, slightly cool. But not cold. And it needs to be filtered. Well, purified. Actually, I need to know the pH level. It should be between 6.5 and 7.5. And please tell me where the water came from. I prefer water from a spring. A specific spring. In Switzerland. Actually, not Switzerland — that's too fancy. Maybe Vermont? No wait, the water needs to be alkaline. But not too alkaline. This is really important for my health and my body's optimal functioning.

jokes.text: I'm very glad that you agree with me, but I actually worked really hard on my argument. Is there any way I can still kind of... / Yeah? / I'd love to hear it.
jokes.setup: Leslie prepared an argument but Chris already agreed
jokes.punchline: She still wants to give her speech despite winning
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie moment - can't waste a prepared presentation even when unnecessary

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, you're not going to argue with me? You're just going to agree?

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Yeah, I think you're right.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, I have a 40-slide PowerPoint presentation I've been working on all night, so I'm going to give it anyway.

jokes.text: Can you put a price tag on a child's smile?
jokes.setup: Leslie delivers what should be a rhetorical question
jokes.punchline: The absurdly cliché nature of the argument after the buildup
jokes.explanation: Deliberately clichéd argument that Leslie thinks is brilliant

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Do you know what the problem with this town is?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It's the government.

jokes.text: The government has been shut for two days, and one city employee has tried to schedule 14 meetings with me. Can you guess who?
jokes.setup: Ben poses it as a guessing game
jokes.punchline: Obviously Leslie, confirming her obsessive nature
jokes.explanation: Establishes Leslie's persistence while showing Ben's exasperation

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Guess who spent all night reorganizing her binders by color, thickness, and the emotional resonance of the topic?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That was me. I needed them organized that way.

jokes.text: I have run 10 miles a day every day for 18 years. That's 65,000 miles. A third of the way to the moon. My goal is to run to the moon.
jokes.setup: Chris mentions his running routine
jokes.punchline: His completely impossible goal of running to the moon
jokes.explanation: Perfect Chris character moment - takes fitness to an absurd, impossible extreme

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I've been training for a marathon, but I've decided to set an even more ambitious goal.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I'm going to run to the moon.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: It's 238,900 miles away, which means if I run 50 miles a day, I can make it there in about 4,778 days. That's only 13 years!

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I've already started mapping out my route and I'm thinking of doing it completely chemical-free with a raw food diet.

jokes.text: Pawnee is better than Idaho. / No, Pawnee is not special. / Your department, all the way down here, is not a priority.
jokes.setup: Leslie's civic pride versus harsh budget reality
jokes.punchline: Ben's brutal honesty about their low priority
jokes.explanation: Leslie's optimism crashed against bureaucratic reality

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ben, I've been thinking about the Ramsett Park project. We could really make a difference in this community.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, we're not getting funding for that. We're not even on the radar.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: But it's such a great idea. The people of Pawnee need this.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: The people of Pawnee need a functioning sewer system. You're asking for a park. We're literally below the sewer on the priority list.

jokes.text: You're non-essential. / That is not your call. / I know, it's on your badge.
jokes.setup: Leslie protests being called non-essential
jokes.punchline: Her own badge literally says non-essential
jokes.explanation: Great visual punchline - the badge undercuts her argument perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I am essential! I'm very essential!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, your badge literally says 'non-essential.'

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's just what they put on there. It doesn't mean anything.

jokes.text: This? This isn't me.
jokes.setup: Confronted with her non-essential badge
jokes.punchline: Leslie's weak denial of obvious evidence
jokes.explanation: Weak follow-up to the badge joke, Leslie's denial falls flat

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: This isn't even my badge. I've never seen this before in my life.

jokes.text: I have a resting heart rate of 28 beats per minute. The scientist who studied me said that my heart could pump jet fuel up into an airplane.
jokes.setup: Chris mentions his low heart rate
jokes.punchline: Absurd claim about pumping jet fuel into planes
jokes.explanation: Classic Chris extreme fitness claim taken to ridiculous heights

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: My resting heart rate is 48 beats per minute. That's lower than most Olympic athletes.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's impressive, Chris.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Thank you. In fact, I'm so efficient that I've been pumping my own blood into the jets at the airport. Saves the city thousands on fuel.

jokes.text: I'd love to go out on a date with you. I think you might find me attractive because you got drunk and kissed me when we first met. And no pressure, but I do have tonight open.
jokes.setup: Chris's overly direct approach to asking Ann out
jokes.punchline: His complete lack of subtlety while claiming 'no pressure'
jokes.explanation: Chris's awkward directness creates cringe comedy

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Ann, I have calculated that you are 94% compatible with me based on my analysis of your physical attributes, career trajectory, and potential for bearing healthy offspring. I would like to take you to dinner. No pressure though.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Chris, that's... that's a lot of pressure.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I'm confused. I explicitly said no pressure. My tone was very casual.

jokes.text: Traditionally, when I end a long-term relationship, I'm a little fragile, and I have a tendency to do some reckless things. So I need to stay away from Chris.
jokes.setup: Ann explains why she avoids Chris
jokes.punchline: Calling the incredibly fit, positive Chris 'reckless'
jokes.explanation: Ironic that the supremely healthy Chris is her dangerous option

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I avoid Chris because he's reckless. He does these crazy things like eat a salad for lunch and go to bed at a reasonable hour.

jokes.text: You know, they offered me a buyout, and I took it. And so now, I work for Norton Construction.
jokes.setup: Mark casually mentions major life change
jokes.punchline: He's now working for what appears to be a private contractor
jokes.explanation: Mark's casual delivery of major news fits his detached character

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: I'm working for a private contractor now.

jokes.text: Mark Brendana-quitz.
jokes.setup: Leslie has been saying Mark's name wrong
jokes.punchline: She turns his name into 'quits' since he's leaving
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay connecting his departure to his name

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Mark, I just realized I've been saying your name wrong this whole time. It's actually pronounced 'Quits,' isn't it? Because you're quitting!

jokes.text: And I was indoors.
jokes.setup: Mark lists bad things that happened, ending with bird poop
jokes.punchline: The impossibility of indoor bird poop makes it worse
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery of the most absurd detail

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: I got bird poop on me. Indoor bird poop. That's impossible.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: Which somehow makes it worse.

jokes.text: Why else would Boyz II Men's On Bended Knee be playing right now?
jokes.setup: Leslie deduces Tom is about to have sex
jokes.punchline: Her evidence is the specific song choice
jokes.explanation: Specific pop culture reference as evidence for romantic activity

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Tom, you're about to have sex.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: What? No, I'm not.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You're playing Marvin Gaye. Nobody plays Marvin Gaye unless they're about to have sex. It's a scientific fact.

jokes.text: Okay. I just want to give you a little advice, which is don't make it last really long. Ladies don't like that.
jokes.setup: Leslie offering unsolicited sex advice
jokes.punchline: Her well-meaning but inappropriate wisdom
jokes.explanation: Leslie's inappropriate oversharing creates cringe comedy

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Look, I'm just going to say it. When you're with a man, you need to take charge. Be aggressive. Grab him by the—well, you know. And don't be shy about telling him what you want. Men respond to confidence.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Also, never let him see you eating. Men don't like that. Just consume nutrients intravenously before dates.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: And wear something that shows you're fun, but not too fun. You want to seem like you might do something crazy, but you won't actually do it. It's a delicate balance.

jokes.text: Leslie's usually already up and often she's already on her way over.
jokes.setup: Ann describes being there for friends at night
jokes.punchline: Leslie is so intense she's already coming over
jokes.explanation: Perfect characterization of Leslie's excessive enthusiasm

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I'm always there for my friends at night.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm so intense I'm already coming over.

jokes.text: The only guy that I care about right now is six feet tall with wild, crazy hair and a ukulele that doubles as a water gun.
jokes.setup: Leslie seems to be describing a romantic interest
jokes.punchline: She's describing Freddy Spaghetti, the children's entertainer
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection - sets up romantic expectation then reveals absurd truth

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: She's describing Freddy Spaghetti, the children's entertainer

jokes.text: Pawnee's kids are less important than poop tubes.
jokes.setup: Leslie compares budget priorities
jokes.punchline: Her crude but accurate assessment of sewage vs. children's programs
jokes.explanation: Crude but effective way to highlight budget priorities

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Look, I know it sounds terrible, but we have to prioritize the sewage system over the children's programs. It's just math.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Poop has to go somewhere. Children are optional.

jokes.text: Ann, you devious bastard.
jokes.setup: Ann suggests having the concert at her house
jokes.punchline: Leslie's over-the-top reaction to a simple suggestion
jokes.explanation: Leslie's dramatic reaction to Ann's helpful suggestion

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ann, that's... that's the most brilliant idea anyone has ever had in the history of ideas. Your house? Of course! Why didn't I think of that? You're a genius. A beautiful, smart genius. I'm going to throw you a parade. Actually, two parades. One after the other. We'll shut down the entire city.

jokes.text: A, yes. B, even if they don't, we'll just put the concert on ourselves. But C, yes. Yes, they're definitely going to show up. Although, D, maybe not.
jokes.setup: Leslie organizing her thoughts with letters
jokes.punchline: She contradicts herself multiple times while staying organized
jokes.explanation: Leslie's need to organize everything, even her contradictions

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, first of all, I think waffles are a perfectly acceptable dinner. Second of all, and I cannot stress this enough, waffles should never be eaten for dinner.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Third, government spending is out of control and needs to be cut. Fourth, we need to immediately increase funding for all local parks and recreation departments.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Fifth, I am completely objective and unbiased. Sixth, anyone who disagrees with me is wrong and probably a bad person.

jokes.text: God bless you, Jerry Gergich!
jokes.setup: Jerry shows up to help
jokes.punchline: Leslie's shock at saying something nice to Jerry
jokes.explanation: Moment of sincerity that surprises Leslie herself

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, did I just say something nice about Jerry? Oh my God, I need to sit down.

jokes.text: I still might not.
jokes.setup: Leslie assumes April is there to help
jokes.punchline: April's brutal honesty about her uncertain commitment
jokes.explanation: Perfect April deadpan - present but uncommitted

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: April, I'm so glad you're here to help with this!

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm not here to help. I'm just here.

jokes.text: My doctor just said I should avoid... / Being a wuss? Yes, I agree.
jokes.setup: Jerry starts to mention doctor's orders
jokes.punchline: Leslie cuts him off and insults him instead
jokes.explanation: Classic Leslie steamrolling Jerry's reasonable concerns

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: My doctor says I should—

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Jerry, your doctor also thinks you're a human being worth listening to, so clearly his medical degree is from a very stupid hospital.

jokes.text: I am an official member of a task force dedicated to slashing the city budget. Just saying that gave me a semi.
jokes.setup: Ron describes his role on the budget committee
jokes.punchline: He's literally sexually aroused by cutting government
jokes.explanation: Peak Ron Swanson - his anti-government stance taken to crude extreme

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: He's literally sexually aroused by cutting government

jokes.text: Right off the bat, we sell City Hall. Let somebody turn it into a large gas station or a T.J. Maxx.
jokes.setup: Ron suggests selling government property
jokes.punchline: His specific suggestions are deliberately unglamorous
jokes.explanation: Ron's anti-government stance expressed through mundane retail suggestions

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: We should sell some government property. I suggest we start with the old quarry, that pit of raw sewage behind the rec center, and whatever that smell is coming from the Parks Department bathroom.

jokes.text: Hammering away.
jokes.setup: Andy is presumably working on construction
jokes.punchline: His overly dramatic narration of simple hammering
jokes.explanation: Andy's tendency to narrate obvious actions

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: And here we are, folks, at the construction site. I'm about to hammer this nail. It's a big moment. The nail doesn't know it yet, but its life is about to change forever. I'm picking up the hammer now. Feel the weight of it in my hand. The responsibility. I'm raising it up... up... the nail is waiting, wondering what will happen next. And now... I'm bringing it down with the force of a thousand suns!

jokes.text: He better be showered. I just detailed my car, and he is notoriously funky.
jokes.setup: Tom agrees to pick up Freddy Spaghetti
jokes.punchline: His concern for his car over helping with the concert
jokes.explanation: Tom's vanity about his car vs. community service

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. Freddy Spaghetti? That's a real person?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: No, no, no. I just got my car detailed. I can't have some guy named Freddy Spaghetti eating marinara in my Lexus.

jokes.text: What were we doing in between? Sex stuff.
jokes.setup: Tom mentions dinner and breakfast with girlfriend
jokes.punchline: His unnecessary oversharing about intimate details
jokes.explanation: Tom's compulsive oversharing creates cringe moment

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: So I took my girlfriend to this amazing restaurant last night. Best dinner ever.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: And then this morning we had breakfast in bed together. If you know what I mean.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: And by that I mean we literally had breakfast in bed. Eggs, toast, the whole thing. It was very intimate.

jokes.text: You guys know Tom really well, so I don't have to apologize for his behavior, right?
jokes.setup: Lucy has just met Tom's coworkers
jokes.punchline: She immediately assumes they're used to his inappropriate behavior
jokes.explanation: Lucy's immediate understanding of Tom's social awkwardness

jokes.text: Oh, I forgot. I didn't get a chance to get porta-potties, so the audience are gonna have to use Ann's house for the bathrooms.
jokes.setup: Leslie mentions a logistical oversight
jokes.punchline: 300 strangers will use Ann's personal bathroom
jokes.explanation: Leslie's casual mention of major imposition on Ann

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I just realized we have a logistical problem. We're expecting 300 people at the harvest festival and we only have one bathroom.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: What? Where are they going to go?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, I was thinking your house is pretty close by...

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: No! Absolutely not. 300 strangers are not using my personal bathroom.

jokes.text: Yeah, it's just like driving half a car.
jokes.setup: Andy explains motorcycle riding
jokes.punchline: His completely nonsensical analogy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy logic - makes no sense but sounds confident

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Riding a motorcycle is like... it's like a woman, you know? You gotta treat it with respect, keep your hands steady, and if you don't wear protection, you're gonna have a bad time. Also, sometimes it leaks fluid and you have to take it to a guy named Pedro.

jokes.text: I was too busy not wanting to talk to you.
jokes.setup: Andy asks why April didn't return his calls
jokes.punchline: April's brutally honest response
jokes.explanation: Peak April deadpan cruelty delivered with perfect timing

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Why didn't you return my calls?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I didn't want to talk to you.

jokes.text: How much do you like him? Because I could definitely talk to Lucy about a three-way situation.
jokes.setup: Tom is being sweet about Ann deserving a good guy
jokes.punchline: Immediately ruins it with inappropriate suggestion
jokes.explanation: Tom immediately undermines his sweet moment with crudeness

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ann, you deserve a good guy. Someone who treats you right, respects you, and makes you happy.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: You know what? I know a guy. He's really into you. He's got a good job, he's stable, he's—well, he's me. I'm suggesting we have sex.

jokes.text: Massage train. And I know what you're thinking. It's not that I want a massage. I'll be the caboose. And Ron Swanson is the locomotive.
jokes.setup: Chris suggests a team-building exercise
jokes.punchline: His overly detailed and inappropriate workplace suggestion
jokes.explanation: Chris's inappropriate workplace wellness suggestion

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Okay, I have a great idea for team building. We're all going to pair up and do a trust fall, but here's the twist—we're going to do it blindfolded, and the person catching you will be behind you, so you have no idea where they are or if they'll actually catch you. Then, we're going to go back to my place and do some trust exercises in a hot tub.

jokes.text: Sell the zoo animals. / To whom? / Cosmetics labs? Weird restaurants? I'm just spit-balling here.
jokes.setup: Ron suggests selling zoo animals
jokes.punchline: His disturbing suggestions for potential buyers
jokes.explanation: Ron's extreme anti-government stance leads to dark suggestions

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: We could sell the animals to a circus.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Ron, no.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: A testing facility?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Ron!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: A restaurant?

jokes.text: Reverse psychiatry.
jokes.setup: April is using psychology on Andy
jokes.punchline: She gets the term completely wrong
jokes.explanation: April's confident misuse of psychological terms

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm using reverse psychology on you.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What do you mean?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm telling you the opposite of what I want so you'll do what I actually want.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That's... that's just psychology.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: No, it's reverse psychology.

jokes.text: It's because whenever I see you talk to Ann or talk about Ann, I feel like you still have feelings for her.
jokes.setup: April explains why she won't date Andy
jokes.punchline: Her mature insight into Andy's unresolved feelings
jokes.explanation: April shows unexpected emotional intelligence

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Andy has unresolved feelings about Ann. He's not over her. Until he figures that out, he's just going to keep dating people who remind him of her in some way. And that's not fair to whoever he's with.

jokes.text: Because of my libertarian beliefs, Leslie does 95% of the work. So you should lay me off. I'd be proud to be a casualty in this righteous war.
jokes.setup: Ron argues for his own firing
jokes.punchline: He calls budget cuts a 'righteous war' while admitting laziness
jokes.explanation: Ron's ideology perfectly aligns with his laziness in hilarious way

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: These budget cuts are a righteous war against government waste.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: And I intend to fight it with every ounce of my being.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Or, at the very least, I would if I wasn't so lazy.

jokes.text: No other department has one to begin with.
jokes.setup: Chris says every department is losing a Leslie Knope
jokes.punchline: Ben points out Leslie is unique
jokes.explanation: Ben's growing appreciation for Leslie's uniqueness

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Well, actually, Leslie is unique. There's only one Leslie Knope.

jokes.text: What? Who? The Russians?
jokes.setup: Someone shouts 'They're coming!'
jokes.punchline: Leslie's mind immediately jumps to Cold War threats
jokes.explanation: Leslie's paranoid escalation to international threats

dialogue.speaker: Unknown
dialogue.line: They're coming!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, are the Russians here? Did Gorbachev finally make his move? I knew it. I KNEW IT. We should have invested in more bunkers. Why didn't anyone listen to me?

jokes.text: When he would do it by himself, nothing got done, and when I did it by myself, I'd get death threats.
jokes.setup: Ben explains their partnership approach
jokes.punchline: His casual mention of receiving death threats
jokes.explanation: Ben's matter-of-fact delivery about serious consequences

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I get death threats all the time. It's just part of the job.

jokes.text: Actually, Leslie, I just found out. Freddy Spaghetti ain't coming. / Freddy Spaghetti may not sing. But something much cooler is going to happen. I think. Ann?
jokes.setup: Bad news about the main performer
jokes.punchline: Leslie's false confidence immediately deflated
jokes.explanation: Leslie's optimism deflating in real time

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: The main performer just cancelled. He's sick.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's fine. I can do it. I'm very talented.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Leslie, you can't even carry a tune.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well... how hard could it be?

jokes.text: At a library. / That's literally the worst place I can imagine.
jokes.setup: Freddy Spaghetti is performing at a library
jokes.punchline: Leslie's dramatic overreaction to a normal venue
jokes.explanation: Leslie's government worker bias against libraries

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: This is the most beautiful venue I've ever seen. The acoustics, the lighting, the way the books are arranged by the Dewey Decimal System... it's perfect. I'm going to cry. Actually, I'm already crying.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Leslie, it's a library.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: A LIBRARY, Tom. A library.

jokes.text: Well, just take the word 'sex' and change it to 'pickle.' / You got pickle hair, baby And you got it from me / Yeah. Still sounds like it's about sex.
jokes.setup: Leslie suggests changing inappropriate lyrics
jokes.punchline: The substitution doesn't help at all
jokes.explanation: Great example of how some lyrics can't be fixed

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We need to change the inappropriate lyrics in this song.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Okay, what do you want to change?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: How about we replace 'sexy' with 'esty'?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: 'Esty'? That's not a word.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It's better than sexy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Is it though? 'You're my esty lady'? That doesn't help at all.

jokes.text: There's an old saying in show business. 'The show must go wrong.' Everything always goes wrong, and you just have to deal with it.
jokes.setup: Andy quotes a show business saying
jokes.punchline: He gets the famous phrase completely wrong but makes it accidentally wise
jokes.explanation: Andy's mangled wisdom that's accidentally profound and funny

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You know what they say in show business: 'It's not about the destination, it's about the friends you make along the way.'

jokes.text: Freddy Spaghetti couldn't make it today. He bumped his noodle. There was sauce everywhere. It was just streaming out of his face.
jokes.setup: Leslie explains Freddy's absence in character
jokes.punchline: Pasta-themed medical emergency description
jokes.explanation: Leslie staying in pasta character while explaining absence

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Freddy couldn't make it tonight. He's in the hospital with a severe case of linguine syndrome.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: The doctors say if we don't get him on a strict penne-cillin regimen immediately, he could develop full-blown fettuccine fever.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It's touch and go, but we're staying optimistic. The prognosis is... al dente.

jokes.text: My father told me that a limp handshake was for weak men and Communists. He hated both.
jokes.setup: Ron compliments Lucy's handshake
jokes.punchline: His father's political paranoia about handshakes
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron family background - paranoid masculinity

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That's a firm handshake. My father always said a firm handshake is the mark of an honest man.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Of course, he also believed that the government was using handshakes to inject tracking devices into our wrists, so...

jokes.text: They call it a Swanson.
jokes.setup: Someone asks about bacon on his turkey leg
jokes.punchline: The vendor named the dish after Ron
jokes.explanation: Ron's meat obsession has achieved local fame

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have no idea what you're talking about. That's just a turkey leg with bacon on it.

jokes.text: My life is a motorcycle crash right now.
jokes.setup: Andy talks about his bad week
jokes.punchline: His metaphor is literally what just happened to him
jokes.explanation: Andy's metaphor is accidentally perfect given his literal crash

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: This week has been crazy. It's like I was a horse, and someone just shot me in the head.

jokes.text: A-Cakes.
jokes.setup: Andy mentions Ann used to call him A-Cakes
jokes.punchline: Ann actually says it, showing their past intimacy
jokes.explanation: Sweet callback to their past relationship

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: A-Cakes.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh my god, you remember that?

jokes.text: Mean Ben? / No, no, no. Mean Ben?
jokes.setup: Leslie apparently calls Ben 'Mean Ben'
jokes.punchline: Ben's hurt reaction to the nickname
jokes.explanation: Ben's genuine hurt at being seen as mean

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Mean Ben? That's what you call me?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, yeah. When you're being all serious and bureaucratic.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I have spent years trying to earn your respect, and this is what I get? Mean Ben?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It's not a bad thing! It's like... it's sexy.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: It is not sexy. It's mean.

jokes.text: The biggest service was getting you to stop singing.
jokes.setup: Ben talks about providing services
jokes.punchline: His brutal honesty about Leslie's singing
jokes.explanation: Ben's mean but funny takedown of Leslie's performance

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: As a government official, I believe in providing services to the public.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: But Leslie, your singing voice is not one of those services.

jokes.text: But, like, two minutes ago, me and Ann did kiss.
jokes.setup: Andy tries to be honest with April
jokes.punchline: His terrible timing in confessing to kissing Ann
jokes.explanation: Andy's well-intentioned honesty backfires spectacularly

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: April, I need to tell you something. I kissed Ann.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: When?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: A while ago. Before we were together.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: So you're telling me this now... why?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Because I want to be honest with you. I love you and I don't want secrets between us.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Andy, I'm pregnant.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: ...I also kissed her that one time last week.

jokes.text: Dude, don't even think about kissing me.
jokes.setup: Tom sees Andy's romantic troubles
jokes.punchline: His preemptive rejection of any comfort
jokes.explanation: Tom's paranoia about male affection

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Don't. I don't want to hear it. I know what you're going to say, and I don't want to hear it.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I wasn't going to say anything.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Exactly. That's the worst part.

jokes.text: And what was your favorite song, Chelsea? / Penne and the Jets. / Me, too!
jokes.setup: Leslie interviews a child about the concert
jokes.punchline: Child turns Elton John song into pasta pun
jokes.explanation: Cute wordplay from child character

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: So what did you think of the Elton John concert?

dialogue.speaker: Child
dialogue.line: It was great! My favorite song was 'Tiny Dancer.' But I kept thinking it should be called 'Tiny Dancer... with Linguine!'

jokes.text: I liked this a lot better when it was a pit.
jokes.setup: Mark looks at the developed lot
jokes.punchline: His preference for the original empty pit
jokes.explanation: Perfect Mark cynicism and callback to series origins

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: You know what? I liked it better as a pit.

jokes.text: Red tape. So you'll never forget your roots.
jokes.setup: Leslie gives Mark a going-away present
jokes.punchline: Literal red tape as metaphor for bureaucracy
jokes.explanation: Clever literal interpretation of bureaucratic metaphor

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Mark, I got you a going-away present. It's a roll of red tape.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: Red tape?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It's a metaphor for bureaucracy. You know, all the red tape you have to cut through in government.

jokes.text: Whenever Ron has sex, the next morning, he comes in dressed like Tiger Woods.
jokes.setup: Ron appears in golf attire
jokes.punchline: Tom's specific observation about Ron's post-coital fashion choices
jokes.explanation: Weirdly specific observation that's both gross and hilarious

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ron, why are you dressed like you just had sex at a country club?

jokes.text: That is my ex-wife.
jokes.setup: Someone appears that makes Ron uncomfortable
jokes.punchline: The reveal that it's his ex-wife
jokes.explanation: Classic setup for Ron's romantic complications

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I need to sit down.

dialogue.speaker: Character
dialogue.line: Ron, what's wrong?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That's my ex-wife. Tammy Two.

tmdb_overview: When Ben prevents Leslie from holding a children's concert, she takes matters into her own hands. Mark makes some big decisions about his life, and Andy gets some unexpected romantic attention.
insight: Absurdist character comedy anchors a 68-joke finale that underperforms on impact despite solid density.
